passages.explanation: When Jesus comes down from the mountain, great multitudes follow him. A leper comes, kneels before him, and says: Lord, if you are willing, you can make me clean. Jesus stretches out his hand, touches him, and says: I am willing. Be cleansed. Immediately the leprosy is cleansed. Jesus tells him: tell no one, but go show yourself to the priest and offer the gift Moses commanded, as a testimony to them. Lepers were untouchable — contact made you ceremonially unclean. Jesus touches him anyway. The cleanness flows from Jesus to the leper, not the other way around.

A centurion in Capernaum approaches Jesus on behalf of his paralysed servant who is suffering terribly. Jesus offers to come heal him, but the centurion says: Lord, I'm not worthy for you to come under my roof. Just say the word and my servant will be healed. I understand authority — I tell one soldier go and he goes, another come and he comes. Jesus is amazed and says to those following: I haven't found such great faith even in Israel. Many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the Kingdom — but the sons of the kingdom will be thrown into outer darkness. He tells the centurion: go, and let it be done for you as you have believed. The servant is healed that very hour. A Roman soldier understands Jesus' authority better than Israel does. Matthew uses this to signal a theme: the kingdom is breaking open beyond ethnic boundaries.

Jesus enters Peter's house and finds Peter's mother-in-law lying sick with a fever. He touches her hand, the fever leaves, and she gets up and serves him. That evening, they bring many who are demon-possessed to him. He casts out the spirits with a word and heals all who are sick. Matthew connects this to Isaiah 53:4 — he took our infirmities and bore our diseases. The healings aren't interruptions to the mission; they are the mission. Isaiah's suffering servant doesn't just deal with sin — he bears sickness.

When Jesus sees great multitudes around him, he gives orders to go to the other side of the lake. A scribe comes and says: teacher, I'll follow you wherever you go. Jesus replies: foxes have holes and birds have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head. Another disciple says: Lord, let me first go bury my father. Jesus says: follow me, and leave the dead to bury their own dead. Jesus doesn't recruit with comfort — he warns. Following him costs more than most people calculate upfront.

Jesus gets into a boat and his disciples follow. A violent storm hits the sea — waves are crashing over the boat. Jesus is asleep. The disciples wake him: save us, Lord, we're dying! He says: why are you fearful, you of little faith? He gets up, rebukes the winds and the sea, and there's a great calm. The men marvel: what kind of man is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him? In the Old Testament, only God controls the sea. The disciples are asking the right question — they just don't have the answer yet.

Jesus arrives in the region of the Gadarenes. Two demon-possessed men come out of the tombs — so violent that no one can pass that way. They cry out: what do we have to do with you, Jesus, Son of God? Have you come to torment us before the time? A large herd of pigs is feeding nearby. The demons beg: if you cast us out, send us into the pigs. He says: go. They come out and enter the pigs. The whole herd rushes down the cliff into the sea and dies in the water. The herdsmen flee and tell the city everything. The whole city comes out to meet Jesus — and they beg him to leave their region. The demons know exactly who Jesus is. The townspeople would rather keep their pigs than deal with that kind of power.
